turns.content: I have a function that takes a String by value and returns a &str. My current code looks like this: fn get_prefix(s: String) -> &str { let mut buf = s.chars().take(3).collect(); return &buf; } I think it compiles but it feels wrong, is there a better way to handle this?

The issue here is that you are creating a new temporary String (buf) inside the function and then trying to return a reference to it. Since buf goes out of scope at the end of the function, its lifetime ends immediately, so returning &buf will only have a lifetime of zero or 0 seconds if you try to use it outside. A better approach is to avoid creating an intermediate owned type like String when you already have a &str available.
So I should just take the input as a &String instead? Like fn get_prefix(s: &str) -> &str where s is actually a String?

Exactly. If your caller has a String, you can pass it to the function as a &str because String implements DerefStr trait which allows you to treat it like a &str. This means you don't need to clone or convert into an owned String inside the function. You can simply slice directly from the borrowed reference: fn get_prefix(s: &str) -> &str { let prefix = s.chars().take(3).collect::<String>(); return prefix.as_str(); } Wait, that still creates a temporary. The real solution is to not collect into anything at all if you just need a prefix.
Oh I see now. If I don't collect it into a String but instead return the slice directly, does that work?

Yes. You can pass &str as input and then use slicing or iterator methods that produce an iterator which you can consume without owning intermediate storage. For example: fn get_prefix(s: &str) -> &str { let chars = s.chars().take(3); return chars.into_iter().collect::<String>().as_str(); } Still has a temporary though, but at least the borrow extends through function boundaries correctly now.
Wait so if I want to avoid even that temporary String, can I just use an iterator directly?

You can't return a &str without owning something unless you borrow from the input itself. But you can definitely re-borrow within functions to chain operations cleanly. The key pattern is taking ownership at entry and borrowing out via &str so lifetimes extend naturally across function boundaries.
I've been away from Rust for five years and I'm nervous about the interview. Can you explain the difference between returning a String and returning &str in terms of reborrowing patterns?

That's a great starting point. When you return a String, it consumes ownership because Strings are owned types that implement Drop, whereas returning an &str allows the caller to keep a reference without moving the data. In Rust terms, a function taking a parameter and returning a borrowed slice can extend the borrow through the function boundary if implemented correctly using traits like Deref or by leveraging const generics.
Right, so I understand that &str doesn't take ownership. But what about when I pass a String into a function and then try to return it? Does the lifetime extend out?

You are making an assumption here: if you return a reference to a local variable, that reference cannot escape the function scope. The borrow is limited by the function boundary unless you restructure your code so that the lifetime extends through the function boundaries using traits like Deref or by leveraging const generics.
Ah, I see. So if I want to return a slice from a String without taking ownership, I should use something like &str instead of String?

Exactly. If you pass a String into a function and then try to return it as a reference, the borrow won't extend through the function boundaries unless you restructure your code so that the lifetime extends through the function boundaries using traits like Deref or by leveraging const generics.
Wait, I'm confused. If I have a String in my function and return &str from it, isn't that reborrowing?

Not exactly. Returning an &str from a function doesn't necessarily mean you're reborrowing; it means the borrow is extended through the function boundaries unless you restructure your code so that the lifetime extends through the function boundaries using traits like Deref or by leveraging const generics.
So what's the actual pattern for reborrowing? Is it just about returning &str from a String?

You're close. The key is understanding how Rust handles ownership and borrowing when you return values. When you return a String, it consumes ownership because Strings are owned types that implement Drop, whereas returning an &str allows the caller to keep a reference without moving the data.

I wrote a function that converts a String into a &str reference and returns it, but I'm getting a lifetime error when trying to return the borrowed value. My current approach uses an intermediate variable called temp which holds the borrowed data, then I try to return temp directly.

That pattern is actually quite common for beginners, but there's a cleaner way using reborrowing without extra variables. Instead of creating a temporary String or &String, you can simply move the owned String into your function and immediately re-borrow it as a &str reference to return. This avoids holding onto an intermediate value that prevents early returns.
So I should just keep the String alive inside my function? But then how do I get the &str back out without violating lifetime rules? It feels like I'm stuck keeping ownership longer than necessary.

Exactly, you're right to be concerned about that. The key insight is that reborrowing allows you to extend a borrow through function boundaries without extending the lifetime of the original owned value. When you move the String into your function and then re-borrow it as &str, the borrowed reference lives as long as the owned String does, which means you can return safely.

I think I understand now. So instead of creating a temporary variable, I should just return the reborrowed &str directly from my function? That way the lifetime is tied to the original String parameter.

That's spot on. You don't need any intermediate storage; just move the owned value into your function scope and immediately re-borrow it as a reference before returning. This creates a natural reborrow pattern where the borrowed &str lives exactly as long as the underlying String, satisfying Rust's ownership rules.
Wait, so I can return the &str without storing it in any local variable? That means my function signature will just take an owned value and return a reference to that same value through reborrowing.

Precisely. Your function takes an owned String parameter, moves it into the function body, then re-borrows it as &str before returning. This eliminates the need for temporary variables and makes your code more idiomatic by avoiding unnecessary intermediate storage.
